sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=19, col=18), MOUSE(row=18, col=30).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 36, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking green(19,18) or green(19,24) spawns 4 g diamonds + 1 G marker. Clicking green(19,36) or right-panel greens resets. Clicking g diamond spawns character. After character spawns, need to figure out what to do - maybe click remaining g diamonds.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 35 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see! The current state has:
- N at (18, 29) - this is a NEW green cell that wasn't there before!
- N at (18, 35) - original green cell
- N at (24, 41) - original green cell  
- N at (36, 41) - original green cell
The original green cell at (19, 18) is gone, and a new green cell appeared at (18, 29). Looking at the ascii, the green cells in the left panel are now at columns 29-32 and 35-38 (which were previously gray cells).
So the mechanic is:
1. Click a green cell → it becomes a G marker, and g diamonds appear around a gray cell
2. Click a g diamond → the gray cell becomes green, and the G marker disappears (reverting to... gray? or the original green cell disappears?)
Wait, let me reconsider. The original green cell at (19, 18) disappeared. A new green cell appeared at (18, 29). The g diamonds were around (19, 30) which is near (18, 29). So clicking the g diamond "transferred" the green from the clicked cell to the target gray cell!
So the game is: move green cells from their starting positions to target positions by:
1. Click the source green cell (becomes G marker)
2. Click the g diamond at the target position (transfers green there)
The goal is probably to move all green cells to specific target positions. Let me figure out what the target configuration is.
Initial green positions: (19,18), (19,24), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42)
After moving (19,18) → (18,29): green at (18,29), (19,24), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42)
Wait, but (19,24) is still green. And (19,36) is still green. So I moved one green cell from (19,18) to (18,29).
The g diamonds appeared at [(18, 30), (19, 29), (19, 32), (21, 30)] when I clicked green(19,18). These surround the position (19, 30) which is a gray cell. But the new green cell is at (18, 29), not (19, 30).
Hmm, let me look more carefully. The new green N cell is at top-left (18, 29). Looking at the ascii:
Row 18: `WwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWWwNNwWWwNNwWWwwww`
The green cells are at cols 29-30 (wNNw pattern) and cols 35-36 (wNNw pattern).
So the target position for the g diamond click was the gray cell at around (19, 30). The g diamond at (18, 30) is the top part of the diamond shape surrounding that gray cell. Clicking it transferred the green to that gray cell.
Now I need to figure out where each green cell should go. The g diamonds indicate the target positions. Let me try clicking green(19,24) to see where its g diamonds appear.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: The game is about moving green cells. Click a green cell → it becomes G marker with g diamonds around a target gray cell. Click a g diamond → transfers green to that target. Need to figure out the correct target positions for all 5 green cells.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current green cells: (18,29), (19,24), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42)
# Let me try clicking green(19,24) to see where its g diamonds appear
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 24}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After clicking green(19,24):")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'g', 'G', 'p'):
        boundary = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: top-left=({min_r},{min_c})")

# Get g diamond positions
g_positions = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'g':
        boundary = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in boundary)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in boundary)
        center_r = (min_r + max_r) // 2
        center_c = (min_c + max_c) // 2
        g_positions.append((center_r, center_c))
g_positions.sort()
print(f"g diamond centers: {g_positions}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After clicking green(19,24):
  N: top-left=(18,29)
  N: top-left=(18,35)
  N: top-left=(24,41)
  N: top-left=(36,41)
g diamond centers: []
